text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Continuing from our study of Numbers 7, we now come to the last Levite division mentioned in the distribution of wagons and oxen: the **Kohathites**. The earlier groups received wagons and oxen because their burdens were bulky and heavy. But for the Kohathites, Scripture says no wagons and no oxen were given.
The reason is very clear. The Kohathites carried the **most sacred objects** of the tabernacle, and their burden had to be carried on their **shoulders**. It was too holy to be placed on carts. It had to be borne personally, with reverence and careful obedience.

The Kohathites were entrusted with the holy furniture inside the tabernacle and certain holy items from the outer court. These were not common objects.
These were the furnishings connected to God’s presence and the service of worship. Their list included the **Ark of the Covenant**, the **table of showbread**, the **lampstand**, the **golden altar**, the **utensils of the sanctuary**, and also the **bronze altar** with its utensils. They carried the things that stood at the center of Israel’s worship and the center of God’s dwelling among His people.

But here is what makes the Kohathite work different from the others.
The Kohathites were not allowed to touch these holy objects directly. They could not walk in and handle them as they pleased. First, **Aaron and his sons**, the priests, had to go in and cover every sacred object for travel. Only after the priests prepared and covered the holy things could the Kohathites come near to lift and carry. If the Kohathites touched the holy things uncovered, they would die. This is stated clearly in **Numbers 4**, and especially the warning. The priests did the covering; the Kohathites did the carrying. Everything was done in strict order because holiness cannot be handled carelessly.

The first item prepared was the **Ark of the Testimony**, also called the Ark of the Covenant. When the camp was ready to move, Aaron and his sons took down the inner veil; the veil that separated the Most Holy Place - and they used that veil itself to cover the ark. Then they placed an outer covering of skins over it, and above that they spread a cloth of pure blue. After these layers were in place, they set the poles so the ark could be carried. This is described in **Numbers 4:5-6**. The ark was wrapped in layers, then carried by poles on the shoulders. It was not dragged. It was not rolled. It was lifted. The weight of holiness was borne.

Next the priests prepared the **table of showbread**. They spread a blue cloth over the table. Then they arranged the dishes, pans, bowls, and pitchers used for drink offerings, and even the regular bread remained on it. Over these they spread a scarlet cloth, and then an outer covering of durable skins. After that, they placed its poles for carrying. This is written in **Numbers 4:7-8**. Even the table was not handled casually. The vessels and bread were arranged under instruction. The coverings were layered in the exact way God commanded. Then it was lifted and carried.

Then came the **lampstand**. The priests wrapped the lampstand, its lamps, wick trimmers, trays, and oil vessels with a blue cloth. Then they covered the whole thing with skins and placed it on a carrying frame. This is in **Numbers 4:9-10**.
The lampstand and its tools were treated as one sacred unit, covered carefully and transported securely.

After that, the priests prepared the **golden altar**, which is generally understood as the altar of incense. They spread a blue cloth over it, then covered it with skins, and inserted its poles. This is written in **Numbers 4:11**. In the same way, all the utensils of ministry in the sanctuary were gathered, wrapped in a blue cloth, covered again with skins, and placed on a carrying frame, according to **Numbers 4:12**. Every holy tool was treated with care. Nothing was treated as common.

Then the priests prepared the **bronze altar**, the altar of burnt offering in the courtyard. They removed the ashes, spread a purple cloth over it, placed all its service tools on that cloth; the firepans, forks, shovels, basins, and all the utensils; and then covered everything with skins and inserted its poles. This is described in **Numbers 4:13-14**. Even the altar that stood outside still had to be prepared and wrapped before transport.

Only after all these items were fully covered and the poles were set could the Kohathites come near to carry. Their job was not to decide the method of covering. Their job was to lift what the priests had already prepared. This is why no wagons were given to them. These holy burdens had to be carried on shoulders. It was personal. It was careful. It was reverent. It was not rushed. And it was not casual. Everything was done under God’s command because these were not ordinary objects. They were the furnishings of God’s dwelling.

So today, the Kohathites stand before us as a picture of holy burden-bearing. They carried the ark, the table, the lampstand, the altars, and the sacred tools, but only after the priests covered them. They carried on their shoulders, not on wagons, because holiness must be borne with personal reverence. What belongs to God must be approached with fear of God, handled with obedience, and carried with careful honor. This carries a deep lesson for the New Creation believers, and we will study in the coming parts.

title: Prayer
text:
As a father has compassion on his children, so You have compassion on us, Yahweh; we come near with reverence and trust.

Keep our hearts from casual worship and careless handling of Your truth. Teach us obedience and order, so we approach You in the way You have commanded. Give us clean hands and a clean conscience, so we do not touch holy things with an unholy life. Help us carry the burdens You assign with faithfulness and fear of God. May Your presence remain honored among us, and may our lives stay set apart for Your dwelling. In Christ name, amen.
